title: feat: XDG base directory support for config (Issue #46)
description:
## Feature: XDG base directory support

### Source: Issue #46 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Support XDG Base Directory Specification for config location. Currently hardcoded to ~/.mempalace. Should respect XDG_CONFIG_HOME, XDG_DATA_HOME, XDG_STATE_HOME.

### Rust implementation
Use dirs crate which already supports XDG:
- Config: dirs::config_dir() / mempalace (XDG_CONFIG_HOME)
- Data: dirs::data_dir() / mempalace (XDG_DATA_HOME) for palace storage
- Fallback: ~/.mempalace for backward compatibility
- Migration path: detect old ~/.mempalace and offer to migrate

### Acceptance criteria
- Respects XDG_CONFIG_HOME on Linux
- Uses ~/Library/Application Support on macOS
- Uses AppData on Windows
- Backward compatible with ~/.mempalace
- Migration from old path works

### Test obligations
- Unit test: path resolution per platform
- Unit test: XDG env var override
- Unit test: fallback to ~/.mempalace
close_reason: Completed XDG base directory support with env var overrides, platform fallbacks, backward compatibility, migration path, and unit tests

title: fix: Windows Unicode console crash in searcher (Issue #47)
description:
## Bug: UnicodeEncodeError on Windows cp1252 console

### Source: Issue #47 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
searcher.py uses Unicode box-drawing chars that crash on Windows cp1252 console. Need ASCII fallback or UTF-8 enforcement.

### Rust implementation
In Rust this is less of an issue since we control output encoding. But still:
- Use ASCII-safe characters for box drawing (-, |, +) or detect terminal capabilities
- Use owo-colors or colored crate which handles terminal detection
- Consider crossterm for cross-platform terminal handling
- Fallback to ASCII when terminal doesnt support Unicode

### Acceptance criteria
- Search output renders on Windows cmd.exe
- Search output renders on Windows PowerShell
- Search output renders on Unix terminals
- No panic on encoding errors

### Test obligations
- Unit test: output formatting with ASCII fallback
close_reason: Already completed previously

title: Verify and align MCP tool schemas: params, defaults, response shapes
description:

title: feat: Codex CLI JSONL normalizer (PR #61)
description:
## Feature: OpenAI Codex CLI JSONL normalizer

### Source: PR #61 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
6th normalize format — parses OpenAI Codex CLI session files from ~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*.jsonl. Each line is a JSON event with timestamp, type, and payload.

### Key event types
- session_meta — session ID, cwd, model, git info
- event_msg/user_message — real user prompts
- event_msg/agent_message — real assistant replies
- response_item — SKIPPED (contains synthetic context, duplicates)

### Design decisions
- Only event_msg entries extracted (not response_item)
- session_meta header required for format detection
- Avoids duplicate turns from response_item entries

### Rust implementation
Add _try_codex_jsonl() to normalize.rs. Parse JSONL line-by-line with serde_json. Filter by event type. Same pattern as other normalizers.

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects Codex JSONL format via session_meta presence
- Extracts user_message and agent_message turns
- Skips response_item entries
- Produces standard transcript format

### Test obligations
- Unit test: parse valid Codex JSONL
- Unit test: reject non-Codex JSONL
- Unit test: handle missing session_meta
close_reason: Already completed previously

title: Port knowledge_graph.py → src/knowledge_graph.rs
description:
## Port: knowledge_graph.py (385 lines) → src/knowledge_graph.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py

### What it does
Temporal entity-relationship graph stored in SQLite. Supports time-filtered queries, fact invalidation (valid_from → valid_to), confidence scores, and source tracking. Core structured knowledge layer alongside the vector palace.

### Key structures to port
- KnowledgeGraph struct with SQLite connection
- add_entity(name, type, properties) — insert entity
- add_triple(subject, predicate, object, confidence, source) — insert relationship
- invalidate(triple_id) — set valid_to timestamp
- query_entity(name) — get entity with relationships
- query_relationship(subject, predicate) — find matching triples
- timeline(entity) — chronological fact history
- stats() — graph statistics
- seed_from_entity_facts(facts) — bulk import

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- sqlite3 → rusqlite
- json → serde_json
- pathlib → std::path
- datetime → chrono

### Rust considerations
- rusqlite is the standard SQLite binding — well-maintained
- Use rusqlite::params! macro for parameterized queries
- Define Triple and Entity structs with serde derives
- Temporal queries use WHERE valid_to IS NULL for current facts
- Consider connection pooling (r2d2-sqlite) if concurrent access needed

### Acceptance criteria
- Creates SQLite schema on init
- Entities stored and retrieved correctly
- Triples with temporal validity work
- Invalidation sets valid_to correctly
- query_entity returns entity + active relationships
- timeline returns chronological history
- stats() reports accurate counts

### Test obligations
- Unit test: add/query entity
- Unit test: add/query triple
- Unit test: invalidation
- Unit test: temporal filtering
- Unit test: timeline ordering
- Unit test: stats accuracy
- All tests use in-memory SQLite (:memory:)
close_reason: Ported knowledge_graph.py to knowledge_graph.rs with full SQLite temporal entity-relationship graph, add_entity/add_triple/invalidate/query_entity/query_relationship/timeline/stats methods, proper temporal filtering, and comprehensive tests.

title: port: safer repair command survives HNSW index corruption (upstream PR #239)

Reduce batch sizes to prevent OOM.

title: Project scaffold: Cargo.toml, src/lib.rs, module structure
description:
## Port: Project Infrastructure

Set up the Rust project skeleton that all other modules will build on.

### What to create
- Cargo.toml with workspace dependencies:
  - chromadb (or equivalent vector DB client — investigate qdrant-client or chromadb Rust bindings)

  - serde + serde_json for JSON handling

### Rust module layout
src/lib.rs, main.rs, config.rs, normalize.rs, miner.rs, convo_miner.rs, searcher.rs, layers.rs, dialect.rs, knowledge_graph.rs, palace_graph.rs, mcp_server.rs, cli.rs, onboarding.rs, entity_registry.rs, entity_detector.rs, general_extractor.rs, room_detector_local.rs, spellcheck.rs, split_mega_files.rs

Every other bead depends on this. The module structure must be in place before any file can be ported. Choosing the right crate equivalents for Python dependencies (chromadb, yaml, autocorrect) is a critical early decision.

- All modules declared in lib.rs
- Dependencies compile
- Error type defined in a shared location

### Test obligations

title: fix: security hardening across modules (PR #34)
description:
## Fix: Security hardening

### Source: PR #34 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Security hardening across 6 files from full codebase audit: shell injection prevention, SSL bypass removal, error leak prevention, read-only MCP mode.

### Rust implementation considerations
Many Python-specific issues dont apply to Rust (no shell injection via subprocess, no SSL bypass). But the principles carry over:
- No shell command execution with user input (use Command::new, not shell strings)
- No sensitive data in error messages
- Read-only MCP mode: separate read/write tool sets
- Input validation on all MCP tool parameters
- MD5 usedforsecurity=false equivalent: document non-crypto usage in comments

### Acceptance criteria
- No shell injection vectors
- Error messages dont leak internal paths or data
- MCP read-only mode available
- All user inputs validated

### Test obligations
- Unit test: input validation on MCP tools
- Unit test: error messages dont contain sensitive data
- Test read-only MCP mode
close_reason: Rust port already immune to Python shell injection/SSL bypass issues. Most security concerns in PR #34 don't apply - Rust has no shell subprocess, no SSL bypass vectors. Specific items handled: input validation exists via serde, error messages use thiserror without path leakage.

title: CI/CD + release workflow + install.sh (/release)
description:
## CI/CD Pipeline + Release Workflow + curl-pipe Installer

### Source: /release skill (rust-cli-scaffold)

### What it does
Add GitHub Actions CI, cross-platform release workflow, and production-grade install.sh to the Rust project. Three files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml — fmt + clippy + test on 3 OS matrix
- .github/workflows/release.yml — cross-compile 5 targets on vX.Y.Z tag
- install.sh — curl-pipe installer with checksum verification

### CI Workflow (ci.yml)
- Trigger: push to main/dev, PRs to main
- Matrix: ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest
- Steps: checkout, rust-toolchain@stable (with rustfmt, clippy), rust-cache, cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -D warnings, cargo test --all-features

### Release Workflow (release.yml)
- Trigger: vX.Y.Z tag push
- Build matrix (5 targets):
  - x86_64-unknown-linux-musl (cross)
  - aarch64-unknown-linux-musl (cross)
  - x86_64-apple-darwin (native)
  - aarch64-apple-darwin (native)
  - x86_64-pc-windows-msvc (native)
- Artifacts: .tar.gz (Unix) or .zip (Windows) + .sha256 sidecar
- Release: softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with generate_release_notes

### install.sh Features
- Safety: set -euo pipefail, umask 022
- Platform detection: Linux/macOS/Windows, x86_64/aarch64
- Version resolution: GitHub API with redirect fallback
- Download: retry + resume + proxy support
- Checksum verification: SHA-256 sidecar
- Atomic binary installation
- Concurrent install locking
- --easy-mode PATH update
- --from-source fallback (cargo build)
- --uninstall flag
- --verify self-test
- curl|bash safety wrapper (buffer entire script)

### Acceptance criteria
- CI passes on all 3 OS
- Release builds all 5 targets
- install.sh works on Linux, macOS, Windows (Git Bash)
- Checksum verification passes
- --from-source builds correctly
- --uninstall removes cleanly
- README updated with install command

### Test obligations
- CI workflow runs successfully
- Release workflow produces all 5 artifacts
- install.sh tested on Linux (at minimum)
- Checksum verification tested
- Uninstall tested

### Final step
After all tests pass:
git tag v0.1.0 && git push origin main --tags
close_reason: CI/CD workflows (ci.yml + release.yml), install.sh with checksum verification and --easy-mode, pushed to main

title: Add contradiction detection in Rust knowledge graph
description:
## Goal
Add contradiction/auto-invalidation detection to the Rust knowledge graph.

## Python behavior (`knowledge_graph.py`)
- `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`
- `valid_from`/`valid_to` temporal validity windows on triples
- `add_triple()` auto-invalidates conflicting triples (same subject+predicate, overlapping validity)
- Consistency enforcement via temporal validity

## Gap
`crates/core/src/knowledge_graph.rs` has no WAL mode, no temporal validity windows, no auto-invalidation of conflicting triples.

## Scope
- Enable `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` on rusqlite connection
- Add `valid_from`/`valid_to` columns to triples schema
- Implement auto-invalidation: on `add_triple`, find conflicting triples (same subject+predicate, overlapping window) and set `valid_to=now()`
- Add temporal query support (`get_valid_triples()` at a point in time)

## References
- Python: `references/mempalace/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py`
- Rust: `crates/core/src/knowledge_graph.rs`
- Parent epic: mr-nrt
close_reason: Aligned KG temporal invalidation and historical query semantics

In knowledge_graph.rs add_triple():

- Insert new triple with valid_from = now
- Log the resolution for audit

- Conflicting triples auto-resolved (old invalidated)
- Resolution logged
- Non-conflicting triples unaffected
- Timeline shows both old and new facts

### Test obligations
- Unit test: add conflicting triple, verify old invalidated
- Unit test: non-conflicting triple preserved
- Unit test: timeline shows history
close_reason: Already completed: Auto-resolve conflicting triples implemented in knowledge_graph.rs add_triple() method. title: MCP server: production-quality Rust MCP implementation (/mcp-builder)
description:
## MCP Server: Production-Quality Rust Implementation

### Source: /mcp-builder skill best practices

### What it does
Build the MCP server (src/mcp_server.rs) following MCP best practices from the mcp-builder skill. This bead covers the quality and architecture aspects beyond the basic port.

### MCP Best Practices to Follow

#### Tool Design
- Clear, descriptive tool names with consistent prefix: mempalace_search, mempalace_kg_query, etc.
- Concise tool descriptions with parameter docs and return type schemas
- Input validation with Zod-equivalent (serde + validation crate)
- Output schemas defined for structured data (not just text)
- Actionable error messages that guide agents toward solutions

#### Tool Annotations (per MCP spec)
Every tool must declare:
- readOnlyHint: true/false (search=true, add_drawer=false)
- destructiveHint: true/false (delete_drawer=true, search=false)
- idempotentHint: true/false (upsert=true, add=false)
- openWorldHint: true/false

#### Transport
- stdio transport for local usage (primary)
- Consider streamable HTTP for remote/multi-agent scenarios
- Stateless JSON for scalability

#### Consolidated Tool Set (14 tools per PR #25)
Read tools: status, list_wings, list_rooms, get_taxonomy, search, check_duplicate, traverse, find_tunnels, graph_stats, kg_query, kg_timeline, kg_stats, diary_read
Write tools: add_drawer, delete_drawer, kg_add, kg_invalidate, diary_write

#### Response Format
- JSON for structured data (search results, stats)
- Markdown for human-readable output (status, help)
- Pagination support for large result sets
- Concise responses — no unnecessary verbosity

#### Error Handling
- Typed error responses per MCP spec
- Actionable suggestions in error messages
- No internal path/data leaks in errors (security bead mr-2ce)

#### Read-Only Mode (PR #34)
- Separate read and write tool registration
- MEMPALACE_READONLY env var to disable write tools
- Useful for shared/public palace access

### Rust Implementation
- Use rmcp crate or implement JSON-RPC over stdio
- Each tool as an async handler function
- Typed input/output with serde
- Tower-based service architecture if using HTTP transport
- tracing for structured logging

### Acceptance criteria
- All 14+ tools registered with proper annotations
- Input validation on all parameters
- Structured output schemas defined
- Error messages are actionable
- Read-only mode works
- stdio transport works with Claude Code
- Tool descriptions are agent-friendly

### Test obligations
- Unit test per tool handler
- Integration test: full MCP initialize + tool call cycle
- Test error responses
- Test read-only mode
- Evaluation: 10 complex questions per mcp-builder Phase 4
close_reason: Implemented with 18 MCP tools, rmcp 1.3, stdio transport, read-only mode, 19 tests passing, pushed to main

title: Deliver exact Python parity for MemPalace Rust port
description:
## Goal
Deliver exact 1:1 functional parity for the Rust port against the Python reference under `references/mempalace-main`.

## Why
This is the umbrella program bead. The repo already has broad module coverage, but the audit found that parity is incomplete in storage/search semantics, conversation mining, CLI surface, MCP surface, config/path behavior, onboarding/entity flows, hooks/instructions, and AAAK behavior. Every implementation bead should optimize for matching Python behavior, not for adding Rust-specific improvements.

## Scope
Treat the Python reference as the behavioral contract for CLI, MCP, mining, onboarding, registry, config, hooks/instructions, and compression. Approved deviations must be explicit and minimal.

## Failure modes
- Agents keep Rust-only UX/API additions that break exact parity.
- Later beads redesign instead of porting semantics.
- Testing verifies internal implementation only, not observable Python-equivalent behavior.

## Testing obligations
This epic is only done when downstream parity beads land and the final parity gate passes.

## Acceptance
- All child beads are closed.
- Final parity ledger enumerates exact matches vs approved deviations.
- The Rust port matches the Python reference for covered scenarios.

title: Resolve exact-parity policy decisions
description:
## Goal
Resolve the policy decisions that can change scope or security posture before implementation starts.

## Why
Several parity areas are blocked by user intent, not just engineering effort. The audit identified five decisions that affect many downstream beads: (1) remove or preserve Rust-only CLI surface like `MineDevice` and `search --embedding`; (2) revert to Python `~/.mempalace` path semantics or preserve XDG behavior as a documented deviation; (3) expose Python-equivalent MCP write tools or keep a safer reduced surface; (4) port Python external entity lookup behavior; (5) include only runtime-equivalent hooks/instructions behavior or also package shell/assets.

## Scope
Produce one written decision record for each policy item and ensure downstream beads reference it.

## Failure modes
- Teams implement different assumptions in parallel.
- Security posture changes without explicit approval.
- Docs/tests encode a behavior the user never approved.

## Testing obligations
No code tests here. Validation is a decision record that unblocks downstream work.

## Acceptance
- Each policy item has an explicit approved outcome.
- Approved deviations are written in language suitable for the final parity ledger.
close_reason: Completed policy decisions

title: Align onboarding prompt flow, defaults, and bootstrap artifacts

title: Match MCP tool catalog, naming, and schema contracts

title: Build final parity gate, report, and approved-deviation ledger
description:
## Goal

## Why
The project cannot credibly claim exact parity without a single place that states what is exact, what is still failing, and what deviations were explicitly approved. This bead is the final verification contract.

## Scope

title: Build authoritative Python↔Rust parity matrix
description:
## Goal
Build the authoritative parity matrix that maps the Rust codebase to the Python reference and the Python test suite.

## Why
Implementation should not start from a vague audit summary. This bead creates the canonical contract for the work: what exists in Python, what exists in Rust, which gaps are missing vs partial vs divergent vs extra, and which tests should prove parity.

## Key references
- Rust CLI/MCP/config: `crates/core/src/cli.rs`, `crates/core/src/mcp_server.rs`, `crates/core/src/config.rs`
- Python CLI/MCP/config: `references/mempalace-main/mempalace/cli.py`, `mcp_server.py`, `config.py`
- Python tests: `references/mempalace-main/tests/*.py`

## Scope
Inventory CLI commands/options, MCP tools/schemas, miner/convo-miner behavior, onboarding, entity registry, hooks/instructions, compression, and config/path behavior. Also map Python test modules to the Rust modules or integration paths they should validate.

## Failure modes
- Later beads discover new scope because the matrix was too thin.
- Test translation happens without a source-of-truth mapping.

## Testing obligations
Review the matrix against the Python test tree and confirm every major reference area is represented.

## Acceptance
- One matrix exists and classifies every major area as exact, partial, missing, divergent, extra, or blocked-by-foundation.
- The matrix explicitly lists which later beads own which gaps.
close_reason: Completed parity matrix

title: Conditionally sync docs and perform final parity acceptance
description:
## Goal
Update docs only where parity work changed user-visible behavior, then perform final release-style acceptance.

## Why
The user asked for docs sync only if implementation changes user-visible behavior. Once the parity gate exists, the project still needs one final pass to ensure README/install/help/setup guidance matches the new truth and that release-readiness is explicit.

## Scope
Diff final behavior against `README.md`, `install.sh`, and any user-visible docs/setup examples; update only necessary sections; then publish a concise final acceptance summary.

## Failure modes
- Unnecessary docs churn hides the real parity work.
- Docs/examples lag behind final CLI/MCP/path behavior.

## Testing obligations
Validate all updated examples against the final CLI/MCP behavior. Confirm the parity gate passes before sign-off.

## Acceptance
- User-visible docs match final approved behavior.
- Final acceptance summary states whether exact parity was reached and lists any approved remaining differences.

title: Port project traversal and ignore semantics into Rust miner
description:
## Goal

## Why
The current Rust miner uses a smaller static skip set and lacks full `.gitignore`-aware behavior. Python project mining treats file selection as part of the public contract. If traversal diverges, later search, duplicate checks, MCP results, and tests all drift.

## Key references
- Rust: `crates/core/src/miner.rs`
- Python: `references/mempalace-main/mempalace/miner.py`

## Scope
Port file discovery semantics including readable extension set, skip file/dir behavior, maximum file size handling, and `.gitignore` + include-ignored semantics as needed for exact parity.

## Failure modes
- Under-implementing ignore precedence.
- Matching only happy-path files while hidden/cache/generated files still diverge.

## Testing obligations
Add traversal-focused regression tests using Python-equivalent fixtures and path edge cases.

## Acceptance
- Rust project mining chooses the same files Python would mine for covered scenarios.
- Ignore and include rules behave equivalently in tests.

title: install.sh: auto-install MCP config into user's providers
description:
## Feature: Auto-detect and auto-install MCP server config into user's AI tool providers

### Reference implementation
File: /home/quangdang/projects/aw/agents-auth/install.sh (lines 82-266)

### What it does
After building/installing the mempalace binary, automatically detect which AI coding tools the user has installed and inject the MCP server config into each one. Zero manual config editing required.

### Supported providers (from agents-auth reference)
| Provider | Config path | Config key | Format |
|----------|------------|------------|--------|
| Claude Code | ~/.claude.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Codex | ~/.codex/config.toml | mcp_servers | TOML |
| Cursor | ~/.cursor/mcp.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Windsurf | ~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| VS Code | .vscode/mcp.json | servers | JSON |
| Gemini | ~/.gemini/settings.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| OpenCode | ~/.opencode.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Amp | ~/.config/amp/settings.json | amp.mcpServers | JSON |
| Droid | ~/.factory/mcp.json | mcpServers | JSON |

### Detection logic (from reference)
```
detect_hosts():
  ~/.codex/ exists → codex
  ~/.claude.json exists → claude-code
  ~/.cursor/ exists → cursor
  ~/.codeium/windsurf/ exists → windsurf
  .vscode/ exists → vscode
  ~/.gemini/ exists → gemini
  ~/.opencode.json exists → opencode
  ~/.config/amp/ exists → amp
  ~/.factory/ exists → droid
```

### Config injection pattern (from reference)
For JSON configs: read existing file → navigate to servers key → upsert "mempalace" entry → write back. Entry format:
```json
{"command": "/path/to/mempalace", "args": ["mcp"]}
```

For TOML configs (Codex): inject [mcp_servers.mempalace] section with command and args.

Status tracking: each install reports "installed" (new), "updated" (changed), or "unchanged".

### Rust implementation
Two places this runs:
1. install.sh — bash script during curl-pipe install (reference pattern already works)
2. mempalace install-mcp CLI subcommand — Rust native for post-install or manual trigger

For the Rust CLI subcommand:
- detect_providers() → Vec<Provider> — check filesystem for known paths
- install_mcp_config(provider, binary_path) → InstallResult — upsert config
- Use serde_json for JSON configs, toml crate for TOML configs
- Atomic writes (write to .tmp then rename)
- Preserve existing config (dont clobber user settings)
- Report per-provider status

For install.sh:
- Port the write_json_config() and write_toml_config() Python helpers to pure bash or keep Python (reference uses embedded Python)
- detect_hosts() is already pure bash
- Add mempalace-specific entry format

### CLI interface
```
mempalace install-mcp          # auto-detect and install all
mempalace install-mcp --host claude-code  # specific provider
mempalace install-mcp --list   # show detected providers
mempalace install-mcp --dry-run  # show what would change
mempalace uninstall-mcp        # remove from all providers
```

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects all 9 providers correctly
- JSON config injection preserves existing settings
- TOML config injection preserves existing settings
- Idempotent: running twice produces "unchanged"
- --dry-run shows changes without writing
- --list shows detected providers
- uninstall removes mempalace entries cleanly
- install.sh auto-runs detection after binary install
- Works on Linux, macOS, Windows (Git Bash)

### Test obligations
- Unit test: detect_providers with mock filesystem
- Unit test: JSON config injection (new file, existing file, existing entry)
- Unit test: TOML config injection
- Unit test: idempotency (install twice = unchanged)

description:
## Port: layers.py (507 lines) → src/layers.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/layers.py

### What it does
Implements the 4-layer memory stack: L0 (identity, ~100 tokens), L1 (essential story, ~500-800 tokens), L2 (on-demand wing/room filtered, ~200-500 tokens), L3 (deep search, unlimited). MemoryStack orchestrates all layers.

### Key structures to port
- Layer0 struct — loads identity from config
- Layer1 struct — top palace moments (pre-computed)
- Layer2 struct — wing/room filtered retrieval
- Layer3 struct — full semantic search
- MemoryStack struct — orchestrates all layers
- Methods: wake_up(), recall(), search(), status()

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → vector DB client
- pathlib → std::path
- collections → std::collections
- config module (internal)

### Rust considerations
- Trait-based layer design: trait MemoryLayer { fn recall(&self, query) -> Vec<Memory> }
- MemoryStack holds Vec<Box<dyn MemoryLayer>> or concrete fields
- Token counting approximation (chars/4 or use tiktoken-rs)
- wake_up() composes L0+L1 into a prompt string

### Acceptance criteria
- L0 returns identity text within token budget
- L1 returns top moments within token budget
- L2 filters by wing/room correctly
- L3 performs full semantic search
- wake_up() produces combined context string
- status() reports per-layer statistics

### Test obligations
- Unit test per layer
- Integration test: wake_up with populated palace
- Test token budget enforcement
- Test empty palace handling
close_reason: Fully ported layers.py to src/layers.rs - Layer0/L1/L2/L3/MemoryStack all implemented with 26 unit tests passing

title: perf: batch I/O and hash-set dedup in mining (PR #38)
description:
## Perf: Algorithmic improvements across hot paths

### Source: PR #38 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Two key performance improvements:
1. Batch I/O: accumulate chunks and insert in single batch call per file (was 1 call per chunk)
2. Hash-set dedup: pre-fetch all known source_files in one query, check membership in HashSet (was 1 query per file)

### Rust implementation
Build these optimizations into miner.rs and convo_miner.rs from the start:
- Collect all chunks for a file, then batch-insert via single vector DB call
- Pre-fetch known source files into HashSet<String> before scanning
- Use rayon for parallel file processing if beneficial

### Acceptance criteria
- Mining 500 files uses O(1) dedup queries, not O(n)
- Chunks batch-inserted per file, not per chunk
- Performance measurably better than naive approach

### Test obligations
- Benchmark: mine N files, measure DB calls
- Unit test: batch insert produces same results as individual inserts
close_reason: Already completed - batch I/O and hash-set dedup in mining was done previously

title: Port general_extractor.py → src/general_extractor.rs
description:
## Port: general_extractor.py (522 lines) → src/general_extractor.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/general_extractor.py

### What it does
Extracts 5 memory types from text using keyword/pattern heuristics (no LLM): DECISIONS, PREFERENCES, MILESTONES, PROBLEMS, EMOTIONAL. Segments text, scores markers, disambiguates overlapping types, detects sentiment and resolution.

### Key functions to port
- extract_memories(text) → Vec<Memory> — main entry
- _split_into_segments(text) — break text into logical segments
- _split_by_turns(text) — split by speaker turns
- _score_markers(segment) → HashMap<MemoryType, f64> — keyword scoring
- _disambiguate(scores) → MemoryType — pick best type
- _get_sentiment(text) → Sentiment — positive/negative/neutral
- _has_resolution(text) → bool — check if problem was resolved

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- re → regex crate
- No external dependencies — pure heuristics

### Rust considerations
- MemoryType as enum with 5 variants
- Marker patterns as const arrays of &str
- Scoring is keyword counting + weighting
- Sentiment detection via positive/negative word lists
- Pure functions — easy to test

### Acceptance criteria
- All 5 memory types detected correctly
- Segmentation handles multi-turn conversations
- Disambiguation picks strongest signal
- Sentiment detection works
- Resolution detection works for problems

### Test obligations
- Unit test per memory type with sample text
- Unit test: segmentation
- Unit test: disambiguation with competing signals
- Unit test: sentiment detection
- Unit test: resolution detection
close_reason: done
